summary: DHH celebrates O'Reilly Media's growing support for Ruby on Rails, noting their commitment through multiple book deals, their CTO building an app with Rails, and significant presence at OSCON 2005. He highlights praise from O'Reilly's conference coordinator Nathan Torkington, who called Rails 'astounding,' and from Tim O'Reilly himself, who endorsed the 'extraction over invention' philosophy behind Rails. The post captures an early moment of institutional validation for Rails from one of the most respected names in programming publishing.
key_points: O'Reilly committed to Rails with two proprietary books plus distribution of two Pragmatic Bookshelf titles, signaling serious institutional backing
Rails earned a keynote, session, and tutorial at OSCON 2005, reflecting rapid rise in the open source community
Tim O'Reilly endorsed the 'frameworks are extractions' philosophy — that Rails was extracted from Basecamp rather than designed by committee
O'Reilly's CTO Rael Dornfest was actively building an application in Rails, with DHH personally mentoring him
money_quotes: For as long as I've been dabbling in programming, O'Reilly has been an institution in that good, authority-inducing sense of the word.

Ruby on Rails is astounding. Using it is like watching a kung-fu movie, where a dozen bad-ass frameworks prepare to beat up the little newcomer only to be handed their asses in a variety of imaginative ways.
That's how much I love 'convention over configuration' and the other philosophies behind Rails.
Rails shows us a very interesting future for web applications, and is a great example of innovation from within the open source community.

Ruby on Rails was extracted from basecamp. This approach seems obvious and commonsense — but hardly common in this era of heavy web services standards-ware designed by technical committees far in advance of actual implementation.
key_insight: Institutional validation from respected publishers like O'Reilly signaled Rails' transition from scrappy newcomer to legitimate force in web development.
